provenance.why: RoPE pairing-convention boundary: partial-RoPE dimension pairing at the rope_dim midpoint (half = rope_dim/2) across three independent CPU/NEON decode-step implementations, each of which pairs the wrong two dimensions of the rotated prefix together; the wrong convention diverges by O(0.1-1) from an independent reference versus <1e-4 agreement when the pairing is correct. Each of the three decode paths carries its own inlined copy of the loop, so a fix to one does not carry to the others.

subject: lattice
task_text:
lattice's CPU f16, CPU Q8, and NEON Q8 decode-step attention kernels each apply a partial rotary position embedding (RoPE) to a prefix of every attention head's dimensions (rope_dim out of head_dim). Each of the three implementations carries its own independently inlined copy of the RoPE-application loop, so a fix applied to one does not carry over to the others.

Comparing the post-rotation K (and Q) vectors these three decode paths produce, for the same head and position, against an independently-computed reference shows a systematic divergence on the order of 0.1-1 in the rotated dimensions — far above the well-under-1e-4 agreement expected between two correct RoPE implementations. The divergence is present from the first decoded position onward. It is not a crash and not an out-of-bounds read: both the buggy and the reference computation touch every dimension in the rotated prefix exactly once and consult the same cos/sin table, so the output looks plausible, not obviously garbled — it is simply the wrong pair of dimensions being rotated together.

The boundary that matters is the midpoint of the rotated prefix (rope_dim / 2): whatever rule decides which two dimensions of the rotated prefix get combined into one rotation pair is where the three kernels disagree with the rest of the codebase. Fixing that rule in one of the three kernels does not fix the other two — each has its own inlined copy of the loop.
